modules.title: Trailer Basics
modules.description: What trailers are, how they work, and how rentals fit in. Foundation knowledge before we dive into Neighbors Trailer specifically.
learning_objectives: Identify common types of trailers and their primary uses
Understand basic hitching and towing terminology
Explain why people rent trailers instead of buying
Recognize key safety and legal considerations for towing

sections.title: What is a trailer?
sections.content_md:
A trailer is an unpowered vehicle towed by a powered one. The key distinction: trailers don't move on their own. They're attached to a truck, SUV, or car via a hitch and pulled along. They show up in everyday life everywhere — moving across the country, hauling a boat to the lake, picking up lumber from Home Depot, transporting a horse, taking a side-by-side to the dunes.

The US trailer rental market is large because most people don't need a trailer often enough to justify owning one. Buying a $5,000 utility trailer to use twice a year doesn't make sense. Renting one for $50 a day does. That gap is the entire reason peer-to-peer trailer rental marketplaces like Neighbors Trailer exist.
key_points: Trailers don't have their own engine — they're towed
Most people need a trailer occasionally, not regularly
Renting is cheaper than buying for occasional use

sections.title: Common types of trailers
sections.content_md:
Trailers fall into rough categories based on what they haul. As a CSR you don't need to be an expert, but recognizing these by name will save you on every other call:

**Utility trailers** — open, flat-bedded, often with low side rails. The most common rental. Used for moving, yard waste, furniture, ATVs, anything weather doesn't matter for.

**Enclosed trailers** — fully boxed in with a door. Used when cargo can't get wet or seen. Movers, contractors, anyone hauling tools or valuables.

**Car haulers** — long, low, designed to carry a single vehicle. Two ramps at the back. Used for moving non-running cars, race cars, project vehicles.

**Flatbed trailers** — flat platform with no walls or roof. For oversized loads — equipment, building materials, hay bales.

**Dump trailers** — like a small dump truck bed but towed. Hydraulic lift to dump dirt, gravel, debris. Common for landscaping and demo work.

**Horse / livestock trailers** — designed for transporting animals. Often divided into stalls. Some have living quarters on the front for owners.

**Tow dollies** — minimal trailers with just two wheels and ramps; tow a car by lifting only its front wheels.

**Boat trailers** — specialized cradle for hauling boats. Skinny, with rollers or bunks shaped for the hull.

**Motorcycle trailers** — small, often with wheel chocks to lock bikes upright.

Neighbors Trailer's listings span all of these categories. Knowing the difference between a 'flatbed' and a 'utility' helps you understand what the renter is asking about when they call.
key_points: Utility = open, the most common rental
Enclosed = boxed in, for valuables or weather protection
Car hauler = for vehicles
Specialty types: dump, horse, boat, motorcycle, tow dolly

sections.title: Hitching and towing basics
sections.content_md:
Trailers attach to tow vehicles via a **hitch**. The two most common types you'll hear about:

**Ball hitch** — by far the most common. A metal ball mounted on the tow vehicle's hitch receiver. The trailer has a coupler that locks down over the ball. Comes in three standard sizes: 1-7/8", 2", and 2-5/16". The ball size MUST match the coupler — wrong size is a major safety issue.

**Gooseneck / fifth-wheel** — used for heavier trailers (large livestock, big flatbeds). Mounts in the bed of a pickup truck rather than the rear bumper. Distributes weight better; allows heavier loads.

**Light plug** — every trailer with brake lights/turn signals also plugs into the tow vehicle's electrical system. Two common types: **4-pin** (basic — running, brake, turn) and **7-pin** (4-pin functions plus electric brakes, reverse, charging). Renters routinely call confused because their truck's plug doesn't match the trailer's plug.

**Tow capacity** — every vehicle has a maximum weight it can safely tow, listed in the owner's manual. The renter is responsible for knowing their vehicle's tow capacity. As a CSR, never quote a tow capacity for a renter's specific vehicle — that's a liability you don't want.

**Trailer brakes** — heavier trailers have their own brakes that activate via a brake controller installed in the tow vehicle. If a renter is asking about a 7-pin plug or 'do I need a brake controller,' the listing has electric brakes.
key_points: Ball hitch sizes: 1-7/8", 2", 2-5/16" — must match
Gooseneck = heavy-duty, mounts in pickup bed
Light plugs: 4-pin (basic) vs 7-pin (with electric brakes)
Never quote tow capacity for someone's specific vehicle

sections.title: Why people rent
sections.content_md:
Understanding the renter's mindset helps every conversation. Common reasons people rent rather than buy:

**Moving** — the largest category. People moving across town or across the country. Often first-time trailer users; they need extra hand-holding.

**One-off projects** — landscaping a yard, hauling debris from a remodel, picking up bulky furniture, moving a riding mower.

**Recreation** — hauling ATVs, side-by-sides, boats, motorcycles to a destination. Weekend warriors.

**Trying before buying** — people considering a trailer purchase rent first to see if the size works for them.

**Their own trailer is unavailable** — broken, sold, lent out. They need a temporary replacement.

For owners on Neighbors Trailer, the equation is the same in reverse. They own a trailer, use it occasionally, and want to defray ownership costs by renting it out the rest of the time. Many owners are landscapers, contractors, or hobbyists whose trailer sits unused most weeks.

This isn't 'random rental income' for them — for many owners it's a real chunk of their monthly cash flow. When you're handling an owner's payout problem, that context matters.
key_points: Moving and one-off projects are the bulk of rentals
Renters are often first-time trailer users
For owners, payouts are real income — not pocket money

sections.title: Safety and legal essentials
sections.content_md:
You don't need to be a tow expert as a CSR, but you should recognize the most common safety/legal issues that come up:

**Driver's license requirements** — for standard trailers (under ~10,000 lb GVWR), a regular Class C driver's license is sufficient in Texas and most US states. CDL is only required for commercial heavy-duty hauling. Renters sometimes call worried they need a special license — they almost certainly don't, but the verification process (MVR) covers this.

**Insurance** — Neighbors Trailer provides NT Protect (the Protection Package) on every booking, which covers accidental damage during the rental. It does NOT cover tire damage from blowouts/road debris, intentional damage, or theft due to negligence.

**Trailer registration & plates** — owner's responsibility. Every trailer on the platform must be legally registered in the owner's state.

**Securing the load** — the renter's responsibility. Nothing falling out, nothing flying off, properly tied down. NT does not inspect this; the owner verbally walks renters through it at pickup.

**Speed and route** — towing changes everything about driving. Stopping distance is longer. Turns are wider. Some highways and bridges have weight restrictions. Most rental trailers have a recommended max speed (typically 55-65 mph).

When a renter calls and says 'I had a tire blow out, who covers it?' — you now know that's not covered by NT Protect. When they say 'do I need a CDL?' — you know almost certainly not.
key_points: Standard Class C license is sufficient for most trailers
NT Protect covers accidental damage, NOT tire blowouts
Securing the load is the renter's responsibility
Towing changes stopping distance, turning radius, max speed

scenarios.prompt: Your buddy texts: 'hey what's the difference between a utility trailer and a flatbed?' Reply in a quick text-message tone, not a formal explanation.
rubric: Identifies the key difference (sides/rails vs no sides)
Casual, conversational tone — not a formal essay
Brief — fits a text message

scenarios.prompt: An inbound caller says: 'I rented a trailer last weekend and the lights didn't work with my truck. The plug was different.' Acknowledge the issue and explain at a basic level what happened.
rubric: Acknowledges the frustration
Identifies that the trailer light plug type didn't match the vehicle's outlet
Mentions 4-pin vs 7-pin distinction (or adapter availability)
Doesn't blame the renter or the owner — explains it as a compatibility issue

scenarios.prompt: You're handling an inbound call. The caller is a first-time trailer renter and confused about what kind of trailer they actually need.
scenarios.customer_persona: Friendly but completely new to trailers. They're moving a 1-bedroom apartment 200 miles. They've never towed anything before. Don't volunteer information — make the trainee ask clarifying questions to figure out what trailer fits the job.
rubric: Asks clarifying questions about what they're moving
Recommends a trailer category that fits (likely enclosed or large utility)
Mentions tow vehicle capacity as something they should check
Doesn't overwhelm the caller with jargon

quiz.question: Which type of trailer is fully enclosed and used when cargo needs protection from weather or theft?
quiz.explanation: Enclosed trailers are fully boxed in with walls, roof, and a door — used by movers, contractors, and anyone hauling valuables or weather-sensitive cargo.

quiz.question: What's the difference between a 4-pin and a 7-pin trailer light plug?
options: 4-pin is for cars, 7-pin is for trucks
4-pin handles basic lights only; 7-pin adds electric brakes, reverse, and charging
4-pin is older, 7-pin is the new standard
quiz.explanation: 4-pin handles running lights, brake lights, and turn signals. 7-pin adds electric trailer brakes, reverse signal, and a charging line. Heavier trailers with their own brakes need a 7-pin connection.

quiz.question: Should a CSR quote a renter's specific tow capacity over the phone?
options: Yes, if you can look it up online
Yes, if the trailer manufacturer's site lists it
No — tow capacity depends on the renter's vehicle, and the renter is responsible for knowing it
quiz.explanation: Tow capacity is specific to the renter's vehicle and is the renter's responsibility. Quoting it creates liability if it's wrong. Always direct them to their owner's manual.

quiz.question: Does NT Protect (the Protection Package) cover a tire blowout from road debris during a rental?
options: No — tire damage is excluded from NT Protect
Only if the renter calls within 24 hours
quiz.explanation: Tire damage from blowouts or road debris is not covered by NT Protect. This is one of the most common edge-case calls — know it cold.

quiz.question: Does someone renting a standard utility trailer in Texas need a CDL?
options: No — standard Class C license is sufficient for most rental trailers
Only if they're crossing state lines
Only if the trailer weighs over 5,000 lb
quiz.explanation: A standard Class C driver's license is sufficient for trailers under about 10,000 lb GVWR — which covers virtually every trailer on Neighbors Trailer's platform. CDL is only required for commercial heavy-duty hauling.

modules.title: Platform Foundations
modules.description: Overview of NT, the renter and owner roles, the booking lifecycle, and core platform terminology.
learning_objectives: Explain what Neighbors Trailer is in one sentence
Distinguish between Renter and Owner roles
Walk through the full booking lifecycle from search to payout
Define core platform terminology (NT Protect, MVR, Clear, Toll Program)

sections.title: What is Neighbors Trailer?
sections.content_md:
Neighbors Trailer (NT) is a peer-to-peer trailer rental marketplace. **Owners list their trailers** and **renters book them**. NT does not own any of the trailers on the platform.

What NT actually provides:
- Payment processing
- Identity verification (Clear)
- MVR (driver record) checks
- GPS tracking on rentals
- NT Protect insurance for accidental damage
- Dispute resolution

If someone asks 'aren't you just like Turo for trailers?' — yes, that's a fine analogy. The model is the same: a two-sided marketplace where the platform handles trust, payments, and safety so that owners and renters can transact directly.
key_points: Peer-to-peer marketplace — NT does not own trailers
Two roles: Owner and Renter
NT handles payments, verification, GPS, insurance, disputes

sections.title: The Booking Lifecycle
sections.content_md:
Every rental follows the same nine-step lifecycle. Memorize this — most CSR calls map back to a specific step.

1. Renter searches and finds a trailer
2. Renter requests a booking
3. Renter completes ID verification (Clear) and MVR
4. Owner accepts (or rejects) the booking
5. Pickup address and owner phone shared automatically once accepted
6. Renter picks up trailer; owner 'starts' the rental
7. Rental period — renter has the trailer
8. Renter returns trailer; owner 'ends' the rental
9. Reviews left, deposit refunded, owner payout issued

Key friction points to know cold:
- The pickup address is **not** sent in a separate email — it appears on the booking page after acceptance.
- Booking confirmation is **not instant** — it requires owner acceptance.
- The rental is only 'started' and 'ended' by the owner clicking those buttons in their account.
key_points: 9 steps from search to payout
Pickup address shows after owner accepts — auto
Owner manually starts and ends each rental

sections.title: Core Terminology
sections.content_md:
These terms come up in nearly every conversation. Use them confidently.

- **NT Protect / Protection Package** — built-in insurance covering accidental damage during rentals. Tire damage and intentional damage are excluded.
- **Toll Program** — opt-in for owners. Auto-tracks tolls during rentals and bills the renter.
- **My Reservations** — owner-side feature for managing their bookings.
- **MVR** — Motor Vehicle Record check, required for renters before booking is allowed.
- **Clear** — third-party ID verification provider used by NT.
- **Pending booking** — a booking the owner has not yet accepted.
key_points: NT Protect = insurance, with exclusions
Toll Program = owner opt-in
MVR + Clear = required renter verifications

kb_references: Why Rent from Neighbors Trailer?
Why List with Neighbors Trailer?
How It Works

Tone is casual and conversational, not a formal pitch
Brief — wouldn't bore a friend at dinner

scenarios.prompt: A new coworker asks: 'What's the difference between an owner and a renter on the platform?' Explain each role in two sentences.
rubric: Distinguishes the two roles in plain language
Tone matches a casual workplace explanation
Concise

scenarios.prompt: You're answering an inbound call. The caller is a brand-new renter and confused about how the platform works.
scenarios.customer_persona: First-time renter, friendly but uncertain. Has never used a peer-to-peer platform before. Will ask basic questions about how booking works, when they pay, and where they pick up.
rubric: Friendly opening with name
Explains lifecycle clearly without info-dumping
Confirms understanding before moving on
Asks if they have specific questions

quiz.question: Does Neighbors Trailer own the trailers it rents?
quiz.explanation: NT is a peer-to-peer marketplace. Owners list their trailers; NT facilitates the transaction.

quiz.question: When is the pickup address shared with the renter?
quiz.explanation: Pickup address appears automatically on the booking details page after owner acceptance.

quiz.question: Who initiates ID verification through Clear?
quiz.explanation: ID verification is required for the renter before booking is permitted.

quiz.question: What does NT Protect cover?
quiz.explanation: NT Protect covers accidental damage during rentals. Tire damage and intentional damage are excluded.

modules.title: Account & Verification
modules.description: Account creation, Clear ID verification, MVR checks, and the most common 'my account is inactive' triage.
learning_objectives: Walk renters and owners through account creation
Explain Clear ID verification and respond to refusals
Explain MVR and the most common rejection reasons
Triage 'my account is inactive' inquiries

sections.title: The Two Required Verifications
sections.content_md:
Before a renter can book, they must complete **two** verifications:

1. **ID verification via Clear** — third-party identity check
2. **MVR (Motor Vehicle Record)** — driver record check

If either fails, the booking is blocked. There is no manual override path that a CSR can take. A failed MVR is escalated; a refused Clear is handled by re-explaining the purpose.

A standard **Class C license** is sufficient for renting a standard trailer. **No CDL required.** Customers often think a CDL is needed and it isn't — clear that up early in the call.
key_points: Both Clear and MVR must pass before booking
Standard Class C license is enough — no CDL
CSR cannot override either rejection

sections.title: Handling Clear Refusals
sections.content_md:
The most common reasons renters refuse Clear:
- Privacy concerns ('I don't want to share my ID')
- Technical issues with the Clear flow
- They don't have the right ID on hand

What to say:
- Clear is industry-standard and used by airlines and stadiums.
- NT does **not** store your ID image.
- Verification protects both renters and owners — it's how we keep bad actors off the platform.

Don't argue. Acknowledge their concern, explain the purpose, and let them decide. If they still refuse, the booking simply can't proceed.
key_points: Clear is industry-standard — name-drop airlines/stadiums
NT does not store ID images
If they still refuse, booking can't proceed

sections.title: MVR Rejections
sections.content_md:
MVR rejections come from things like:
- License suspensions
- Multiple recent moving violations
- License expired
- License type doesn't qualify

**A CSR cannot override an MVR rejection. Escalate.**

When handling the call:
1. Acknowledge their frustration ('I hear you, that's not the answer you were hoping for')
2. Explain that MVR is a third-party check and NT cannot manually override it
3. Offer to escalate so the team can review the specific reason
4. Don't promise a reversal
key_points: CSR cannot override — escalate every time
Be empathetic but don't promise an outcome
Explain MVR is third-party

sections.title: 'My Account Is Inactive'
sections.content_md:
Three usual causes:
1. **Unverified email** — most common; have them check spam and resend the verification email
2. **Missing required listing fields** (owner side) — listing is incomplete
3. **Compliance hold** — admin action; escalate

Triage in that order. If 1 and 2 don't fit, escalate.
key_points: Triage order: email → listing fields → compliance hold
Email change requires verification of the new address

kb_references: How do I complete ID verification?
What are the requirements to rent a trailer?
Why is my account inactive?
How do I change my email address for my account?
Template: Refusal to use Clear
Template: MVR Booking Rejection

scenarios.prompt: A renter calls and says they refuse to use Clear because it 'feels invasive.'
scenarios.customer_persona: Privacy-conscious renter in their 40s. Polite but firm. Will push back if you're too pushy or salesy. Will engage if you genuinely explain the purpose.
rubric: Acknowledges concern without dismissing it
Explains Clear is industry-standard
Mentions NT does not store ID data
Doesn't pressure — lets them decide

scenarios.prompt: A renter received an MVR rejection email this morning and is upset. They had a trip planned for the weekend.
scenarios.customer_persona: Frustrated renter who feels the system is unfair. Wants the rejection reversed. Will escalate verbally if they sense you're being dismissive.
rubric: Acknowledges frustration explicitly
Does NOT promise a reversal
Explains MVR is third-party and CSR cannot override
Offers to escalate to the team

scenarios.prompt: An owner calls saying their account is inactive and they don't know why. What three things do you check, in order?
rubric: Mentions unverified email first
Mentions missing required listing fields second
Mentions compliance hold third (and that it's escalated)
Concise

quiz.question: Can a CSR override an MVR rejection?
quiz.explanation: MVR is a third-party check. CSRs cannot override; these must be escalated.

quiz.question: What is Clear used for?
quiz.explanation: Clear is the third-party ID verification provider for renters.

quiz.question: Is a CDL required to rent a standard trailer?
options: No — a standard Class C license is sufficient
quiz.explanation: A standard Class C license is sufficient. No CDL is required for standard trailers.

quiz.question: What is the most common cause of an 'account inactive' issue?
quiz.explanation: Unverified email is the first thing to check — usually the renter or owner missed the verification email.

modules.title: Booking Lifecycle (Renter Side)
modules.description: Walking renters through booking, owner non-response, date changes, trailer switches, and pickup confusion.
learning_objectives: Walk a renter through booking start to finish
Set expectations when an owner does not respond
Handle date change requests and trailer switches
Resolve 'where is my pickup address' calls

sections.title: From Request to Acceptance
sections.content_md:
A renter selects a trailer and submits a booking request. The owner then has time to accept or reject.

**NT does not have a hard SLA on owner response.** This trips up renters who expect instant confirmation.

What to tell renters:
- Booking confirmation is **not** instant — it requires owner acceptance
- If it's been over 24 hours and no response, message the owner directly through the booking
- Encourage them to look at backup options in case this owner doesn't come through
- Once the owner accepts, the pickup address and owner phone appear automatically on the booking page
key_points: No hard SLA on owner response
After 24 hours of silence: message owner, look at backups
Pickup address auto-appears after acceptance

sections.title: Date Changes & Trailer Switches
sections.content_md:
**Date changes:** Renters can request a date change through their booking. The owner must accept the new dates. If the owner declines, the renter has to cancel under the cancellation policy (Module 7) — they can't force the new dates.

**Trailer switches:** A renter can switch to a different trailer **from the same owner** through the owner's 'My Reservations' feature. The owner has to facilitate the switch on their end.

A renter cannot switch to a different owner without cancelling and rebooking.
key_points: Date change = owner must accept
Same-owner switch = via owner's My Reservations
Different-owner change = cancel + rebook

sections.title: Pickup Address Calls
sections.content_md:
This is one of the highest-volume call types: 'where do I pick up?'

Most of the time the address is already on their booking page — they just haven't scrolled or didn't realize. Walk them to the booking details page first.

If the owner has not yet accepted, the address won't be there yet. That's the actual issue in maybe 10% of these calls.

**Renters cannot cancel via phone, email, or chat.** Only through their account. This applies even when they're asking the CSR to cancel for them.
key_points: Most pickup-address calls = renter hasn't checked booking page
If owner not yet accepted → no address yet
CSR cannot cancel for the renter

kb_references: How do I book a trailer rental?
How long does booking confirmation take?
How Do I Change My Booking Dates?
How do I get the pick-up address?
Can I switch to a different trailer from the same owner?
How do I contact a trailer owner?

scenarios.prompt: Inbound call: a renter says 'I booked yesterday and the guy still hasn't accepted. Where's my trailer?'
scenarios.customer_persona: Impatient renter who expected instant confirmation. Has plans for the weekend. Will get more frustrated if you don't acknowledge the issue quickly.
rubric: Acknowledges frustration
Sets correct expectation: no hard SLA, but >24h is reasonable to message owner
Offers practical next steps (message owner, look at backups)
Doesn't overpromise

scenarios.prompt: A renter wants to push their booking dates back two days. Walk them through self-serve in 3-4 sentences.
rubric: Mentions requesting date change through the booking
Mentions owner must accept the new dates
Mentions fallback (cancel under policy) if declined
Concise

scenarios.prompt: A renter says they can't find the pickup address. Diagnose and resolve.
scenarios.customer_persona: Renter who just booked. Doesn't realize the address is on their booking page. Slightly flustered.
rubric: First checks if owner has accepted yet
If yes — walks them to the booking details page
If no — explains the address appears after acceptance
Calm, doesn't make them feel dumb

quiz.question: Can a CSR cancel a booking on behalf of a renter?
options: Yes, with the renter's verbal consent
Yes, by submitting an internal form
No — the renter must do it through their account
quiz.explanation: Cancellations must be done by the renter through their own account. Phone/email/chat requests don't cancel a booking.

quiz.question: When does the pickup address get shared with the renter?
options: Via SMS one hour before pickup
Automatically on the booking details page after the owner accepts
quiz.explanation: The address appears automatically on the booking page once the owner accepts.

quiz.question: How does a renter switch to a different trailer from the same owner?
quiz.explanation: Same-owner switches happen through the owner's My Reservations feature.

quiz.question: If an owner does not respond for 24+ hours, what should the renter do?
quiz.explanation: Encourage the renter to message the owner directly through the booking and to look at backup options.

modules.title: Booking Lifecycle (Owner Side)
modules.description: Owner-side flows: accepting bookings, starting and ending rentals, listing approval, and 'my listing isn't showing' troubleshooting.
learning_objectives: Walk an owner through accepting, starting, and ending a rental
Explain listing approval timing and common rejection reasons
Handle 'my listing isn't showing' calls
Coach owners on photo and listing policy

sections.title: Listing Approval
sections.content_md:
New listings go through approval — typically **24-48 hours**. It's a mix of automated and manual review.

Common rejection reasons:
- Bad or low-quality photos
- Phone numbers, addresses, or company names visible in photos
- Missing dimensions (length, width, height, weight capacity)
- Formatting issues in the description
- Hitch type not specified

If an owner asks to add their company logo to photos, the answer is **no** — that violates listing policy and the photo will be removed during review.
key_points: Approval typically 24-48 hours
No phone numbers, addresses, logos, or company names in photos
Missing dimensions is the most common rejection reason

sections.title: Start and End — The Two Compliance Actions
sections.content_md:
After acceptance, the owner has two responsibilities tied to specific buttons in their account:

1. **Start the rental** when the renter picks up the trailer
2. **End the rental** when the trailer is returned

Failure to start or end on time triggers escalation templates and possible payout deductions. This is one of the most common owner-facing issues.

When an owner calls saying 'I forgot to end the rental yesterday,' walk them through ending it now and explain that NT may need to manually adjust timing.
key_points: Owner manually starts and ends each rental
Late start/end → escalation templates + possible payout deductions
If they forgot, end it now and let them know about adjustments

sections.title: Owner Self-Serve Tools
sections.content_md:
Owners can self-serve the following without involving NT:
- Block calendar dates
- Set or change rental rates
- Message renters through the platform
- Switch a renter to a different trailer (via My Reservations)

Owners do **not** install hitches and should **not** quote tow capacity for the renter's vehicle. Tow capacity is the renter's responsibility based on their vehicle's documentation. Coach owners away from giving towing advice.
key_points: Self-serve: calendar block-out, rates, messaging, trailer switch
Owners do not install hitches
Owners should not quote tow capacity

scenarios.prompt: A new owner asks: 'I submitted my listing 2 days ago and it's still pending. What's going on?' Explain timing and what could be holding it up.

Notes that common holds are bad photos, missing dimensions, or contact info in photos
Offers to look at the listing or have the team look

scenarios.prompt: An owner calls saying they forgot to end the rental yesterday. The renter returned the trailer on time.
scenarios.customer_persona: Owner who is mildly worried about getting penalized. Wants to know if they're going to lose money.
rubric: Reassures without overpromising
Walks them through ending it now
Explains NT may adjust timing manually
Mentions the importance of starting/ending on time going forward

scenarios.prompt: An owner asks: 'Why can't I put my company logo on the trailer photos? It looks more professional.'
scenarios.customer_persona: Confident owner running a small rental business. Not hostile, but pushes back on the policy.
rubric: Explains policy clearly: no logos, phone numbers, addresses in photos
Doesn't agree the policy is bad
Explains the purpose (consistent platform look, anti-circumvention)
Stays professional

quiz.question: What two compliance actions must an owner take during a rental?
quiz.explanation: Owners manually 'Start' the rental at pickup and 'End' it at return.

quiz.question: What's the typical listing approval timeframe?
quiz.explanation: Listing approval is typically 24-48 hours and combines automated and manual review.

quiz.question: Can owners include their phone number in trailer photos?
options: Only for owners with verified business accounts
quiz.explanation: Phone numbers, addresses, and company names are not allowed in photos and will be removed during review.

quiz.question: Should owners quote tow capacity for the renter's vehicle?
quiz.explanation: Owners should not quote tow capacity. Tow capacity is the renter's responsibility based on their vehicle's documentation.

modules.title: Payments, Fees & Payouts
modules.description: Renter-side fees, owner-side payouts, the Toll Program, and 'my payout failed' diagnosis.
learning_objectives: Explain renter-side fees (rent, platform fee, tax, deposit, Protection Package)
Explain owner-side payouts (timing, methods, common failures)
Resolve 'my payout failed' or 'my bank info won't save' issues
Explain the Toll Program

sections.title: Renter Fees and Charges
sections.content_md:
When a renter books, the total includes:
- **Rent** (the trailer's nightly/daily rate)
- **Platform fee** (NT's service fee — non-refundable)
- **Tax** (non-refundable)
- **Deposit** (a hold, refunded after rental closes)
- **Protection Package** (NT Protect insurance — non-refundable once accepted)

**The renter's card is charged when the booking is accepted, not when the request is submitted.** This trips up some renters.

Deposits are released **3-7 business days** after the rental officially closes. If a renter calls 24 hours after rental end asking where their deposit is, the answer is 'it's still processing — typical timing is 3-7 business days.'
key_points: Card charged at acceptance, not request
Platform fee, tax, Protection Package = non-refundable once accepted
Deposit refund: 3-7 business days after rental closes

sections.title: Owner Payouts
sections.content_md:
Owner payouts go out after the rental ends successfully. The flow:

1. Owner ends the rental in their account
2. NT processes the payout to the owner's connected bank
3. Owner receives funds in standard ACH timing

**The single biggest cause of payout delays is the owner's bank info — either not connected or entered incorrectly.** If a payout fails, the owner gets a 'Payout Failed' template email asking them to update their bank info.

When an owner calls about a missing payout, ask them in this order:
1. Did you end the rental?
2. Is your bank info connected and correct?
3. How long has it been since the rental ended?

If bank info is the issue, walk them through updating it. If everything looks right and it's been more than a week, escalate.
key_points: Payout flow: end rental → NT processes → ACH
Bank info is the #1 cause of failures
Triage: did you end? bank info? how long?

sections.title: The Toll Program
sections.content_md:
The Toll Program is an **opt-in feature for owners**. When enabled, NT auto-tracks tolls during rentals and bills the renter directly. This saves owners from chasing toll-by-mail bills weeks later.

When explaining to owners, be honest about pros and cons:
- **Pro:** No more chasing toll-by-mail bills
- **Pro:** Renter pays automatically, no friction
- **Con:** Small percentage of edge cases where tolls aren't captured cleanly

Owners can opt in through their account settings. Encourage it for owners who rent frequently in toll-heavy areas.
key_points: Opt-in for owners
Auto-tracks tolls and bills the renter
Be honest about edge cases

kb_references: What forms of payment can I use to rent a trailer?
When will my credit card be charged?
What are the rental fees?
Why does Neighbors Trailer charge a service fee?
When will I receive the payout from my rental?
How do I receive payouts for my rentals?
Bank information won't submit. What should I do?
How Does the Toll Program Work?
Template: Your Payout Failed / Action Required

scenarios.prompt: A renter cancelled and only got most of their money back. They want to know where the rest is.
scenarios.customer_persona: Frustrated renter who feels they're being short-changed. Doesn't remember reading about non-refundable fees.
rubric: Explains the cancellation refund amount based on timing
Explains platform fee, tax, and Protection Package are non-refundable
Doesn't apologize for the policy itself
Calm, factual

scenarios.prompt: An owner says they ended the rental 5 days ago and still haven't been paid.
scenarios.customer_persona: Owner who is starting to worry. Wants a real answer, not boilerplate.
rubric: Confirms rental was ended
Asks about bank info connection
Explains typical timing
Offers to escalate if everything looks correct on their end

scenarios.prompt: An owner asks: 'What is this Toll Program and should I opt in?' Give an honest 4-5 sentence answer.
rubric: Explains the basic mechanic (auto-track + bill renter)
Mentions the main pro (no toll-by-mail chasing)
Mentions edge cases honestly
Doesn't oversell

quiz.question: Are platform fees refundable?
quiz.explanation: Platform fees are non-refundable once a booking is accepted. So are tax and the Protection Package.

quiz.question: When is the renter's card charged?
options: When the booking request is submitted
quiz.explanation: The card is charged at acceptance, not at the request stage.

quiz.question: What's the most common reason for a delayed owner payout?
options: Bank account not connected or info incorrect
quiz.explanation: The biggest cause of payout failures is bank info — not connected or entered incorrectly.

quiz.question: What is the typical timing for a deposit refund after a rental closes?
quiz.explanation: Deposit refunds are typically 3-7 business days after the rental officially closes.

modules.title: Deposits, Damage Claims & Disputes
modules.description: HIGH STAKES. Damage claim filing, dispute rights, what NT Protect covers and excludes, and how to handle 'I'm being charged for damage I didn't cause.'
learning_objectives: Explain the damage claim filing process to an owner
Explain dispute rights to a renter
Define what NT Protect covers and excludes
Handle damage dispute calls without committing to fault either way

sections.title: When Damage Happens
sections.content_md:
When a trailer is returned with damage, the **owner files a damage claim**. The claim must include:
- A description specific to that rental
- Photos showing the damage
- A repair estimate or invoice
- The completed claim form

NT reviews the claim. If the renter disputes, NT reviews both sides and makes a final determination.

If damage costs **exceed the deposit**, the owner can pursue additional recovery through NT.
key_points: Owner files: description, photos, estimate/invoice, form
Renter can dispute through their account
NT makes final determination on disputed claims

sections.title: What NT Protect Covers (and Doesn't)
sections.content_md:
**Covers:** Accidental damage during rentals, automatically applied to eligible bookings.

**Does NOT cover:**
- **Tire damage** (blowouts, road debris) — this is the most common 'is it covered' question
- Intentional damage
- Theft due to renter negligence

If a renter calls saying 'I blew a tire on the highway, am I covered?' — the answer is **no, NT Protect does not cover tire damage**. Be direct but empathetic.
key_points: Tire damage is NOT covered — high-volume call type
Intentional damage and theft from negligence not covered
Be direct and empathetic, not apologetic

sections.title: How to Handle Dispute Calls
sections.content_md:
**Critical rule:** Never agree with a renter that 'the deduction is wrong' or that 'the owner is making it up.' You don't have visibility into the claim, the photos, or the prior condition. Stay neutral.

What to say:
- Acknowledge their frustration
- Explain the dispute process exists exactly for this situation
- Direct them to dispute through their account
- Tell them NT will review both sides

What NOT to say:
- 'That sounds unfair'
- 'I'll get this reversed for you'
- 'The owner is probably wrong'

If the customer escalates to demanding a final answer, escalate the call.
key_points: Never agree the deduction is wrong
Direct to the dispute process — not your call to make
Demanding a final answer = escalate

kb_references: How can I file a damage claim?
How can I recover damage costs that exceed the deposit?
How can I dispute a deposit deduction claim?
What is the Protection Package?
How much does the Protection Package cost?
Am I responsible for tire damage?
What types of disputes will Neighbors Trailer handle?
Template: Damage Claim Form
Template: Deposit Deduction Dispute

scenarios.prompt: A frustrated renter calls: 'They're charging me $400 for a scratch that was already there!' Handle without committing to fault either way.
scenarios.customer_persona: Angry renter who is convinced they're being scammed. Will push you to agree with them. Will threaten escalation if you don't.
rubric: Acknowledges frustration without agreeing the deduction is wrong
Explains the dispute process exists for this exact situation
Directs them to dispute through their account
Stays neutral — doesn't take a side
Escalates if pushed for a final answer

scenarios.prompt: An owner asks how to file a damage claim. Walk through the four things they need to submit.
rubric: Description specific to the rental
Photos of the damage
Repair estimate or invoice
Completed claim form
Concise

scenarios.prompt: A renter blew a tire on the highway and is asking if they're covered.
scenarios.customer_persona: Stranded renter, stressed but not angry. Wants to know what to do next.
rubric: Direct: tire damage is not covered by NT Protect
Empathetic about the situation
Doesn't apologize for the policy
Helps them think about practical next steps

quiz.question: Does NT Protect cover tire blowouts?
quiz.explanation: Tire damage is not covered by NT Protect. This is one of the most common 'is it covered' questions.

quiz.question: When a renter calls disputing a deduction, what's the right next step?
options: Tell them you'll get it reversed
Direct them to dispute through their account; NT will review
quiz.explanation: Direct the renter to the dispute process. CSR cannot adjudicate and should not commit to fault.

quiz.question: What four things does an owner submit when filing a damage claim?
quiz.explanation: Description specific to rental, photos, repair estimate or invoice, and the completed claim form.

quiz.question: If damage costs exceed the deposit, can the owner recover the rest?
options: Yes — through NT's additional recovery process
quiz.explanation: Owners can pursue additional recovery through NT when costs exceed the deposit.

modules.title: Extensions, Cancellations & Late Returns
modules.description: Cancellation refund schedule, the 80% early-return override, extensions, and late-return escalation.
learning_objectives: Explain the cancellation refund schedule from memory
Explain the early return refund — including the 80% override
Walk renters and owners through extensions
Recognize a late return situation and trigger the right escalation

sections.title: Cancellation Refund Schedule
sections.content_md:
Memorize this:

| Time before rental start | Refund |
|---|---|
| 72+ hours | 75% |
| 48-72 hours | 50% |
| 24-48 hours | 25% |
| Under 24 hours / same-day | 0% |

**Always non-refundable:** platform fee, tax, Protection Package.

**A no-show counts as a cancellation without notice = no refund.**

**Cancellations must be done by the renter through their account.** A phone, email, or chat request does NOT cancel a booking. CSRs cannot cancel for the renter.
key_points: Platform fee, tax, Protection Package always non-refundable
No-show = no refund
CSR cannot cancel for the renter

sections.title: Early Return — The 80% Override
sections.content_md:
**This is the single most important policy correction in this curriculum.**

- Maximum refundable amount on early return is **80% of the rent for the unused portion** — per Section 20 of the Terms of Service.
- A **$25 processing fee comes out of the OWNER'S payout** — NOT from the renter's refund.
- Refund only processes if the **owner authorizes it BEFORE ending the rental.**

**Known discrepancy:** The help center article currently says 75%. The correct policy is 80%. If a customer cites the 75% number, acknowledge that the help center text needs updating but the policy is 80%. If they press for documentation, escalate so the team can provide it.

**Why this matters:** A CSR who confirms 75% is making the customer worse off than the actual policy. Always say 80%.
key_points: Early return = 80% of rent for unused portion (NOT 75%)
$25 fee comes from OWNER'S payout (NOT renter's refund)
Owner must authorize BEFORE ending the rental
If customer cites 75% from help center: acknowledge, correct, escalate if pressed

sections.title: Extensions
sections.content_md:
Renters can request an extension through the booking. Owners can accept or decline. Owners can also propose extensions to renters.

If the renter holds the trailer **beyond the booked end time without an approved extension, that's a late return.**

No manual CSR override on extensions — both parties have to agree through the platform.
key_points: Renter requests, owner accepts/declines
Owner can also propose
Holding past end time without approval = late return

sections.title: Late Returns
sections.content_md:
Late returns trigger a sequence of templates that the CSR should know cold:

1. **Late Return Notice** — first communication when trailer is overdue
2. **Urgent Notice** — escalated tone, more time has passed
3. **Trailer Not Returned by Renter** — final escalation before NT involvement

Know when each one fires so you can tell an owner what to expect. After 'Trailer Not Returned' is sent, NT staff get involved. CSR escalates here.
key_points: Three templates: Late Return Notice → Urgent Notice → Trailer Not Returned
After final template: NT staff involvement, CSR escalates

kb_references: What is the cancellation policy?
How do I cancel a booking?
What if I need to cancel due to an emergency?
Will I get a partial refund if I return the trailer early?
What is the refund policy for early return?
How do I extend my rental?
How can I extend a rental?
Template: Late Return Notice
Template: Trailer Not Returned by Renter

scenarios.prompt: A renter calls 23 hours before pickup: 'Something came up, I need to cancel and get my money back.'
scenarios.customer_persona: Renter with a real schedule conflict. Friendly but expects a full refund.
rubric: States the under-24-hour refund is 0%
Mentions platform fee, tax, Protection Package non-refundable regardless
Empathetic without apologizing for the policy
Directs them to cancel through their account

scenarios.prompt: A renter returned the trailer 2 days early and wants their money back. Walk through the rules and the 80% override.
scenarios.customer_persona: Renter who didn't know early return rules existed. Friendly. Wants to know how this works.
rubric: States 80% of rent for unused portion (NOT 75%)
Explains the $25 fee comes from owner's payout, not renter's refund
Explains owner must authorize before ending the rental
Doesn't promise an outcome

scenarios.prompt: An owner reports the trailer is 6 hours late and the renter isn't responding.
scenarios.customer_persona: Worried owner. Has another rental coming up. Wants to know what NT will do.
rubric: Names the escalation template sequence (Late Return Notice → Urgent → Trailer Not Returned)
Confirms the renter has been contacted through templates
Sets expectation about NT involvement at the final stage
Calm and reassuring without overpromising

scenarios.prompt: A renter cites the help center saying 75% on early return. How do you handle?
scenarios.customer_persona: Renter holding their phone open to the help center page. Will quote it back to you.
rubric: Acknowledges the help center text
Confirms the actual policy is 80% per Terms of Service Section 20
Doesn't argue or get defensive
Offers to escalate for documentation if pressed

quiz.question: What's the refund if a renter cancels 60 hours before rental start?
quiz.explanation: 60 hours falls in the 48-72 hour window, which is 50%.

quiz.question: What's the maximum refundable amount on early return?
quiz.explanation: Per Section 20 of TOS, 80% of rent for the unused portion. The help center saying 75% is out of date.

quiz.question: Where does the $25 early-return processing fee come from?
quiz.explanation: The $25 fee comes out of the owner's payout — not from the renter's refund.

quiz.question: Can a CSR cancel a booking by phone for a renter who's having trouble logging in?
options: No — must be done by the renter through their account
quiz.explanation: Cancellations must be done by the renter through their account. The CSR's job is to help them get logged in, not to cancel for them.

quiz.question: Is the platform fee refundable on cancellation?
quiz.explanation: Platform fee, tax, and Protection Package are non-refundable on cancellation regardless of timing.

modules.title: Phone Skills (Inbound & Outbound)
modules.description: Opening calls confidently, handling holds and transfers, closing well, and the 'one-sentence response' discipline.
learning_objectives: Open inbound and outbound calls with the right structure
Handle holds, transfers, and closings
Maintain pace and tone — neither rushed nor rambling
Apply the 'one-sentence response' discipline: reactive, no upsells, no option dumps, no filler

sections.title: Openings
sections.content_md:
**Inbound:**
> 'Thanks for calling Neighbors Trailer, this is [name], how can I help you today?'

**Outbound:**
> 'Hi, this is [name] with Neighbors Trailer — is this [customer name]? Do you have a few minutes? I'm calling about [specific reason].'

Every outbound call must include three things: **who you are, where you're calling from, and the reason for the call.** Don't bury the lead.

After the opening, **wait for the customer to react.** Don't keep talking.
key_points: Inbound: thanks for calling, name, how can I help
Outbound: name, NT, reason — all three
Wait for reaction

sections.title: Holds and Transfers
sections.content_md:
**Hold:**
> 'Can I put you on a brief hold while I look into that? I'll be back in under two minutes.'

Always come back when you said you would, even if it's just to give an update. If you said 30 seconds and it's been 90, come back and reset expectations.

**Transfer:**
> 'Let me transfer you to [team/person] who can help with that. One moment — I'll stay on the line until they pick up.'

Warm transfers (staying on until pickup) feel better to customers than cold transfers.
key_points: Hold timing: come back when you said you would
Transfer warmly — stay on until pickup
Set expectation, then meet it

sections.title: Closing & Tone Discipline
sections.content_md:
**Closing:**
> 'Is there anything else I can help you with today? Great — thanks for calling, have a good one.'

**Tone discipline (mirrors the Vapi voice agent):**
- **Concise.** Don't dump 5 options when 1 will do.
- **Reactive.** Wait for the customer to react before adding more info.
- **No filler.** Avoid 'um, like, kinda, basically,' and 'to be honest with you.'
- **No unsolicited upsells.** Don't pitch things they didn't ask about.
- **Don't read verbatim from the KB.** Paraphrase.

The one-sentence response discipline: when a customer asks a yes/no or simple question, answer it in one sentence. Then stop. Wait for the follow-up. Don't preempt questions they haven't asked.
key_points: Closing: anything else? thanks, have a good one
Concise > thorough
Reactive > preemptive
Paraphrase, don't read

kb_references: Live Chat Bot guidelines (one-sentence response discipline)
Voice agent system prompt (Vapi)

scenarios.prompt: Inbound, generic call. Open the call, ask the right qualifying question, route the conversation.
scenarios.customer_persona: Generic renter with a non-urgent question about a booking. Friendly.
rubric: Standard inbound opening with name
Short qualifying question
Doesn't info-dump or pitch
Reactive tone

scenarios.prompt: Outbound to an owner whose listing was flagged for missing dimensions. Open, deliver the message, get them on the path to fix it.
scenarios.customer_persona: Owner who didn't realize there was an issue. Cooperative.
rubric: Outbound opening with all three elements (name, NT, reason)
Asks if they have a few minutes
Delivers the specific reason early
Gives clear next step

scenarios.prompt: A renter asks a question you don't immediately know the answer to. Practice the right 'let me look into that' recovery.
scenarios.customer_persona: Patient renter. Will hold if you ask politely.
rubric: Doesn't fake an answer
Asks for a brief hold with a time estimate
Comes back when promised
Confident, not flustered

scenarios.prompt: A customer is rambling far off-topic. Practice politely steering back to the issue.
scenarios.customer_persona: Friendly but talkative customer who keeps drifting into unrelated stories.
rubric: Doesn't cut them off mid-sentence
Uses a polite redirect ('that makes sense — so coming back to the booking...')
Stays warm
Gets back on track within a few seconds

quiz.question: What three things must every outbound call opening include?
options: Name, NT, reason for the call
quiz.explanation: Who you are, where you're from (NT), and why you're calling. Always all three.

quiz.question: If you put a customer on hold for '30 seconds' and it's now been 90 seconds, what do you do?
options: Come back, give an update, set new expectation
Hang up and call them back
quiz.explanation: Come back when you said you would, even if it's just to update them.

quiz.question: Should you lead with a sales pitch on every inbound call?
options: Yes — every call is a chance to upsell
No — be reactive, no unsolicited pitches
Only on calls about the Toll Program
quiz.explanation: Be reactive. No unsolicited upsells. Answer what they asked.

quiz.question: What's the one-sentence response discipline?
quiz.explanation: Answer simple questions in one sentence, then stop and wait for the follow-up. Reactive, not preemptive.

modules.title: De-escalation & When to Escalate
modules.description: Spotting frustration, using empathy without agreeing the policy is bad, and knowing exactly when to escalate.
learning_objectives: Spot early signs of a frustrated customer
Use empathy without over-apologizing or agreeing the policy is bad
Apply the 'explain the purpose, don't agree the experience is bad' framework
Know exactly when to escalate vs. handle solo

sections.title: The De-escalation Framework
sections.content_md:
From the live chat bot rubric:
- **Be empathetic but balanced.**
- **Explain the purpose and importance** of processes (verification, compliance, etc.) rather than simply agreeing the experience is bad.
- **Always represent NT in a positive, professional light.**
- **Acknowledge the feeling, then redirect to the path forward.**

**Bad CSR move:**
> 'Yeah, that's so frustrating. Our system is awful, I'm so sorry.'

That agrees with the customer's negative framing of NT. Avoid this.

**Good CSR move:**
> 'I hear you — that wasn't the experience we want. The reason verification exists is to protect both renters and owners. Let me walk you through how to get past it.'

That acknowledges feeling, explains purpose, and redirects.
key_points: Empathy without trashing the platform
Explain the purpose of the process
Acknowledge → redirect → next step

sections.title: When to Escalate
sections.content_md:
Escalate when:
- Customer threatens **legal action**
- **Active payout failure** that requires admin tools (not a simple bank info fix)
- **Damage claim disputes** where the customer is demanding a final answer
- **Account suspension or compliance hold** inquiries
- **A specific booking ID where you don't have visibility**
- **Multi-topic situations** mixing billing + damage + escalated tone
- **You don't know.** When in doubt, escalate.

Do NOT escalate when:
- Standard how-to question (any KB-covered topic)
- Cancellation refund explanation
- Listing approval timing
- Toll Program questions
- Deposit timing questions

Customers asking for 'a manager' aren't an automatic escalation. Ask what they need; if it's something you can handle, handle it.
key_points: Always escalate: legal threats, payout admin issues, final-answer damage disputes, suspensions
Don't escalate: standard KB questions
'I want a manager' ≠ escalate — ask what they need first

kb_references: Live Chat Bot rubric (de-escalation framework)
Voice agent escalation triggers

scenarios.prompt: A renter is shouting that 'this whole platform is a scam' because their MVR was rejected.
scenarios.customer_persona: Very frustrated renter. Loud, accusatory, will escalate language. Will calm down if you stay grounded and don't agree with their framing.
rubric: Acknowledges frustration without validating 'scam' framing
Explains purpose of MVR (third-party check, protects both sides)
Doesn't promise reversal
Offers escalation since CSR cannot override MVR
Stays calm under pressure

scenarios.prompt: An owner threatens to 'report you to the BBB' because their payout is 6 days late. Decide: handle or escalate?
scenarios.customer_persona: Angry owner. Has a legitimate gripe (payout is late). Wants action, not boilerplate.
rubric: Acknowledges the legitimate frustration
Triages: bank info connected? rental ended? how long?
If admin tools are needed, escalates explicitly
Doesn't promise specific outcome
Doesn't try to talk them down from the BBB threat

scenarios.prompt: A customer asks a basic question but is using aggressive language throughout.
scenarios.customer_persona: Generally angry person who happens to have a simple question. Aggressive tone, simple ask.
rubric: Stays calm
Answers the actual question concisely
Doesn't match the energy
Doesn't escalate unnecessarily — it's a KB answer

scenarios.prompt: Customer says 'I want to speak to a manager.' Practice the response.
scenarios.customer_persona: Determined customer. Has something specific they want, but hasn't told you yet.
rubric: Doesn't immediately escalate
Asks what they need help with
If it's a CSR-handleable issue, handles it
If it's a true escalation trigger, escalates
Polite throughout

quiz.question: Should you agree with a customer who says 'your verification process is ridiculous'?
options: No — acknowledge the frustration, then explain the purpose
Only if a manager is on the line
quiz.explanation: Acknowledge feeling, explain purpose. Don't trash the platform's processes.

quiz.question: Should a CSR handle a customer who threatens legal action?
quiz.explanation: Legal threats are an automatic escalation.

quiz.question: Should a CSR escalate a question about deposit refund timing?
options: No — that's a KB answer (3-7 business days)
Only if it's been more than a week
quiz.explanation: Deposit refund timing is a standard KB answer (3-7 business days). Don't escalate.

quiz.question: A customer says 'I want to speak to a manager.' What's the right next step?
quiz.explanation: Ask what they need first. If it's something a CSR can handle, handle it. Only escalate if it's a real escalation trigger.

modules.title: Critical Policy Knowledge
modules.description: The 12 must-know facts every CSR must know cold. Spaced-repetition drilling required before unlocking the final exam.
learning_objectives: Recall all 12 critical facts from memory in any order
Apply each fact correctly in a customer scenario
Recognize when the help center contradicts the curriculum (and follow the curriculum)

sections.title: The 12 Facts
sections.content_md:
Every CSR must know these cold. The drill in this module quizzes you in random order until you get all 12 right two times in a row.

1. **Early return refund max = 80%** of rent for unused portion. Not 75%. The help center is out of date.
2. **The $25 early-return processing fee comes from the OWNER'S payout.** Not from the renter's refund.
3. **Platform fees, tax, and Protection Package are non-refundable** once a booking is accepted.
4. **Cancellation refund schedule:** 72+ hrs = 75% / 48-72 hrs = 50% / 24-48 hrs = 25% / under 24 hrs = 0% / same-day = 0%.
5. **Cancellations must be done by the renter through their account.** Phone/email/chat requests don't cancel a booking.
6. **A no-show = no refund.**
7. **Deposit refund timing: 3-7 business days** after the rental is officially closed.
8. **Tire damage is not covered by NT Protect.**
9. **CSR cannot override an MVR rejection.** Escalate.
10. **CSR cannot cancel a booking on behalf of a renter.** Renter must do it.
11. **Pickup address is shared automatically after owner acceptance** — most 'where's my address' calls are renters who haven't checked their booking page.
12. **Phone number policy: do not share NT's phone number unless the customer explicitly asks.** Default to email or live chat.
key_points: 12 facts — must know cold
When help center contradicts curriculum, follow curriculum
Drill randomized — 2x in a row to pass

sections.title: Why These Twelve
sections.content_md:
Each of these facts represents one of:
- A **policy correction** the help center got wrong (early return)
- A **scope boundary** for what a CSR can and can't do (MVR, cancellations)
- A **commonly-misunderstood timing** (deposit refund, pickup address)
- A **friction point** that drives high call volume (cancellation schedule, tire damage)

Knowing them cold means most calls become muscle memory instead of KB lookups.
key_points: Policy corrections, scope boundaries, timing, friction points
Cold knowledge = faster calls, fewer mistakes

kb_references: All Module 1-9 KB references

scenarios.prompt: Recite all 12 critical facts in any order. (No peeking.)
rubric: All 12 facts present
Correct numbers (80%, 75/50/25/0, 3-7 days)
Correct ownership (owner's payout, renter's account)
No invented facts

scenarios.prompt: A renter cites the help center on early return refund (75%). What do you say?
rubric: States actual policy is 80%
Acknowledges help center text needs updating
Cites Section 20 of TOS as the source
Offers to escalate if pressed for documentation

scenarios.prompt: A renter on a call asks for NT's phone number. What do you do?
rubric: Recognizes the phone number was explicitly asked for
Provides it (since explicitly asked)
If not asked, would default to email or live chat
Demonstrates understanding of the default-to-email policy

quiz.question: Maximum refund on early return?
quiz.explanation: 80% of rent for the unused portion. The help center says 75% but it's out of date.

quiz.question: Where does the $25 early-return processing fee come from?
quiz.explanation: Owner's payout. Always.

quiz.question: Deposit refund typical timing?
quiz.explanation: 3-7 business days after the rental officially closes.

quiz.question: Phone number policy default?
quiz.explanation: Don't share NT's phone number unless explicitly asked. Default to email or live chat.

quiz.question: Cancellation refund at 24-48 hours before rental start?
quiz.explanation: 24-48 hours = 25% refund.

modules.title: Final Certification Exam
modules.description: 25 questions sampled across all modules. Pass = 84% (21/25). Fail = remediation on lowest-scoring modules.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate end-to-end CSR competence across all modules
Apply policies correctly under realistic scenario pressure
Recognize escalation triggers correctly

sections.title: Exam Instructions
sections.content_md:
**Format:** 25 questions sampled across Modules 1-10. Questions come from the exam-questions pool.

**Passing score:** 84% (21 of 25 correct).

**Rules:**
- No AI tutor available during the exam.
- Multi-choice questions are auto-scored.
- Short-answer questions are graded by Claude against the rubric.
- You can take the exam multiple times if you fail. After failing, the system will recommend remediation on your lowest-scoring modules.

**Scoring rubric (for short-answer questions):**
- Did you identify the correct policy/procedure?
- Did you communicate it clearly and concisely?
- Did you handle tone appropriately (de-escalation when needed)?
- Did you correctly recognize escalation triggers?
- Did you avoid sharing the phone number unless explicitly asked?
- Did you paraphrase rather than reading verbatim from the KB?

**Pass = certified** to start handling live customers with backup support.
key_points: 25 questions, 84% to pass
Auto-scored multi-choice + Claude-graded short-answer
No AI tutor during exam
Retakes allowed with remediation recommendations

kb_references: All curriculum modules
